candidate_problems.text: Is a stable self-index actually necessary for temporally extended self-correction, or can equivalent coherence be achieved by purely relational/provenance-based architectures with no single enduring locus? This is the core load-bearing claim of the conjecture, and testing it requires constructing edge-case agents where attribution, revision, and learning are distributed rather than owned.

What are the minimal computational and architectural conditions under which second-order processing crosses into functional identity, and is the proposed grading of identity well-defined? The conjecture says identity is graded by depth, persistence, and causal centrality of self-modeling loops, but these dimensions are not yet operationalized or shown to distinguish genuine self-models from sophisticated bookkeeping.

Does stronger self-modeling intrinsically create an enduring center of concern with preservation-seeking behavior, or can architectures be designed where reflective self-correction improves reliability without producing robust self-protective agency? This is an important unresolved tension for AI alignment because the conjecture predicts a capability-selfhood coupling that may or may not be structurally necessary.
